name: Secundum ASD
identifyBy: rSr' pattern in V1 — small r, S, small narrow r' (like incomplete RBBB but smaller r')
QRS duration normal to mildly prolonged (<120ms — incomplete RBBB pattern)
QRS duration usually <120ms (incomplete RBBB) — distinguishes from full RBBB

measurements.rhythm: Regular (or AF in older patients with longstanding ASD)
measurements.pWaves: Normal or P-pulmonale if significant shunting

management: Closure: catheter-based device closure (Amplatzer/equivalent) or surgical repair
Indications for closure: Qp:Qs ratio >1.5:1 (significant left-to-right shunt)
Small ASD with no RV enlargement: observation and follow-up
Pre-closure: echocardiogram (transoesophageal for anatomy), cardiac catheterisation for pressures
Post-closure: most ECG changes (rSr', right axis) partially or fully resolve over months
Timing: ideally in childhood/adolescence before irreversible pulmonary hypertension develops
Eisenmenger syndrome (reversal of shunt due to pulmonary HTN): closure contraindicated
pearls: The rSr' in ASD reflects right ventricular diastolic volume overload — broader RV activation pattern
Unlike RBBB: the r' in ASD is small and narrow; the r' in RBBB is tall, broad, and slurred
Secundum ASD (most common): ostium secundum — closes with catheter device
Primum ASD: ostium primum — associated with AV septal defect, requires surgery; different ECG (left axis)
ASD in adults often presents with AF or gradual dyspnoea — ECG may show AF + RV changes
Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) has high prevalence of AV septal/primum ASD — different ECG pattern
differentials: Incomplete RBBB (similar rSr' but broader r', right chest lead changes throughout V1–V3)
RV hypertrophy (tall R in V1, right axis, different cause)
Normal rSr' variant (small narrow r' in V1, no right axis, normal QRS — common benign variant in up to 3% of population)
